coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived carbon dots with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties for the treatment of wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Ju
Tian, Hua
Pan, Lang
Chen, Zihan
Li, Mengting
Ghiladi, Reza A.
Qin, Ziyu
Yin, Xueqiong
coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots (PAPBCDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method, and evaluated their therapeutic efficacy in wound healing in vivo using a Staphylococcus aureus-infected wound mouse model. The multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) were derived from biomass-based polysaccharide (PAP) and betaine (B), and retained the antioxidant and antibacterial activities, respectively, of their starting precursors. In addition to possessing favorable biocompatibility in cytotoxicity studies, the antibacterial activity of the PAPBCDs was 99.38% against Gram-negative bacteria and 90.80% against Gram-positive bacteria, respectively, suggesting that the betaine-derived quaternary ammonium groups in the PAPBCDs were able to electrostatically disrupt bacterial cell membranes leading to pathogen inactivation. ROS scavenging studies demonstrated that the PAPBCDs could effectively scavenge hydroxyl radicals, suggesting that the PAPBCDs could remove intracellular ROS and protect wound tissue from oxidative stress. In vivo studies using mouse models indicated that PAPBCDs could accelerate S. aureus-infected wound healing. These findings offer some interesting ideas for how to design of effective nanomaterials to treat wound infections, and suggest that multifunctional carbon-dot based materials may play a therapeutic role in tissue regeneration and healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibacterial
Anti-inflammatory
Antioxidant
Quaternized carbon dots
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of wound healing in vivo
Antioxidant activity of PAPBCDs in vitro
Cytotoxicity assays and intracellular ROS scavenging
Antibacterial activity of PAPBCDs in vitro
Evaluation of wound healing by PAPBCDs in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Key Science and Technology Program of Haikou City (2021-024), Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (520QN224), College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project (S202210589012), and Key Research and Development Program of Hainan Province (GHYF2022001).

This work was supported by the Key Science and Technology Program of Haikou City ( 2021024 ), Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( 520QN224 ), College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project ( S202210589012 ), and Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province ( GHYF2022001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of PAPBCDs. (a) TEM image and size distribution (inset) of PAPBCDs; (b) HRTEM image of PAPBCDs; (c) FTIR spectra of betaine, PAP and PAPBCDs; (d)-(f) high resolution XPS spectra of the PAPBCDs.
(a) Scavenging of the ABTS•+ radical by PAPBCDs; (b) Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of DEMPO/·OH adducts obtained under varying concentrations of PAPBCDs.

(a) Under UV light irradiation, fluorescence emission spectra of PAPBCDs as a function of varying H2O2 concentration; (b) the dependence of F0/F on the concentration of H2O2 from panel (a); response of PAPBCDs to •OH: fluorescence emission spectra of PAPBCDs after incubation with H2O2 for (c) 0 min, (d) 10 min and (e) 30 min.
(a) Cell viability of RAW 264.7 cells incubated with PAPBCDs for 24 h. (b) Confocal fluorescence images of H2O2-treated Raw 264.7 macrophages co-cultured with PAPBCDs using DCFH-DA as the ROS probe.

Antimicrobial activities of PAPBCDs. (a) Representative agar plate images (PAPBCDs vs. control groups) corresponding to (b) viability by colony counting for E. coli and S. aureus. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01; (c) Confocal fluorescence images of propidium iodide-stained E. coli and S. aureus treated with PAPBCDs; (d) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus treated with PAPBCDs.

In vivo assessment of PAPBCDs for wound healing: (a) Photos of S. aureus-infected mouse wounds taken one, three, and ten days, either with or without PAPBCD therapy; (b) Wound area at different healing times; (c) H&E staining (note: the red arrows represent inflammatory cells, while the black arrows represent fibroblasts); and (d) Images of the mice wound tissue sections after day 10 with or without PAPBCDs treatment in Masson's trichrome staining.

(a) preparation of PAPBCDs,（b) illustration of PAPBCDs in ROS monitoring, antibacterial studies and ROS scavenging (antioxidant studies), and (c) application of PAPBCDs in bacterial-infected wound mouse models.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived carbon dots with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties for the treatment of wound healing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Provincial Fine Chemical Engineering Research Center, Hainan University, Haikou, Hainan, 570228, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hainan Provincial Fine Chemical Engineering Research Center, Hainan University, Haikou, Hainan, 570228, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, North Carolina State University, Raleigh, North Carolina, 27695-8204 USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, North Carolina State University, Raleigh, North Carolina, 27695-8204 USA

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots (PAPBCDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method, and evaluated their therapeutic efficacy in wound healing in vivo using a Staphylococcus aureus-infected wound mouse model. The multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) were derived from biomass-based polysaccharide (PAP) and betaine (B), and retained the antioxidant and antibacterial activities, respectively, of their starting precursors. In addition to possessing favorable biocompatibility in cytotoxicity studies, the antibacterial activity of the PAPBCDs was 99.38% against Gram-negative bacteria and 90.80% against Gram-positive bacteria, respectively, suggesting that the betaine-derived quaternary ammonium groups in the PAPBCDs were able to electrostatically disrupt bacterial cell membranes leading to pathogen inactivation. ROS scavenging studies demonstrated that the PAPBCDs could effectively scavenge hydroxyl radicals, suggesting that the PAPBCDs could remove intracellular ROS and protect wound tissue from oxidative stress. In vivo studies using mouse models indicated that PAPBCDs could accelerate S. aureus-infected wound healing. These findings offer some interesting ideas for how to design of effective nanomaterials to treat wound infections, and suggest that multifunctional carbon-dot based materials may play a therapeutic role in tissue regeneration and healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most serious risks to public health worldwide is bacterial wound infection. (Wei et al., 2021). While conventional therapeutic methods employ antibiotics to combat bacterial infections (Gao et al., 2014; Kai and Wang, 2020), due to their overuse, so-called “superbugs” or microorganisms that are resistant to drugs have emerged. (Kai and Wang, 2020; Levy and Marshall, 2004). In particular, multidrug resistance makes the treatment of bacterial infections harder, more expensive, and, with increasingly alarming trends, ineffective. Recently, an intense focus has been placed on the possibility of treating bacterial infections using antibacterial nanoparticles. As their antibacterial mechanisms that differ from those of conventional antibiotics (Govindaraju et al., 2009; Levy and Marshall, 2004; Mohan and Mala, 2019; Pikula et al., 2020; Tao et al., 2019), antibacterial nanoparticles such as silver, copper oxide, and zinc oxide nanoparticles (NPs) have drawn a lot of attention due of their capacity to combat drug-resistant forms. A number of antimicrobial mechanisms for metal-based nanoparticles have been widely reported, among which include the disruption of native metal homeostasis, increases in oxidative stress due to the production of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) that give rise to protein and/or DNA oxidation. However, because metal-based nanoparticles have the potential to be cytotoxic to human cells, their use still raises some health concerns (Pikula et al., 2020).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a category of carbon-based nanomaterials consisting of a core–shell structure (Chu et al., 2019). As an alternative to metal-based nanoparticles, CDs have emerged as an appealing choice in biotherapeutic applications, including in the possible treatment of bacterial wound infections, due to their favorable biocompatibility, superior hydrophilicity, and tunable surface functional groups (Adam et al., 2022; Ezati and Rhim, 2022; Iravani and Varma, 2020; Kausar, 2019) (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine) that render them amenable to facile surface functionalization (Chu et al., 2019; Iravani and Varma, 2020; Monte-Filho et al., 2019; Sharma and Das, 2019). These characteristics have focused significant attention on CDs and made them attractive targets for therapeutic applications, such as in drug delivery, bioimaging, bio-labeling, and photodynamic therapy (Kang and Kang, 2021; Wareing et al., 2021). Adding to their appeal, various natural starting materials can be used as sources of CDs, and they can be fabricated using both top-down and bottom-up strategies (Sharma and Das, 2019). Some notable examples of CDs produced by simple and sustainable synthetic techniques using natural biomass as precursor starting materials include (Xiong et al., 2017): i) turmeric-derived CDs exhibited variable photoluminescence and potent antibacterial activity against E. coli with negligible cytotoxicity (Wang et al., 2018); ii) CDs synthesized from Carica papaya leaves showed remarkable biological antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities at low doses in vitro (Gudimella et al., 2022); and iii) seaweed-based polysaccharides with low commercial value were utilized in the synthesis of CDs as a green and economical route when compared to other raw materials, yielding CDs that were fluorescent, water-soluble, and biocompatible, although with poor antioxidant activities (Giesbers et al., 2013). Moreover, previous studies have shown that quaternary ammonium-modified CDs possess antibacterial activities against both drug-susceptible (e.g., E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and S. enteritidis) and microorganisms that are multidrug resistant (MDR), such as methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (Li et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2020). These cationic CDs function by interfering with the electrostatic connection between the negatively charged bacterial cells and the positively charged quaternary ammonium groups. This causes bacterial cell membrane and wall breakage, cytoplasmic component leaking, and ultimately pathogen inactivation (Wan and Yeow, 2012).

In light of the aforementioned studies, we surmised that multifunctional CDs possessing a combination of antibacterial, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory activities should be particularly effective for the treatment of bacterial wound infections in vivo. In wound treatment, reactive oxygen species (ROS) is crucial for the healing process. For example, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) can be employed by the human immune system to induce oxidative damage against bacteria, causing their cell membranes, proteins, and DNA to be damaged, which causes the pathogen to become inactive (Yang et al., 2020). In healthy cells, the ROS generated in vivo are eliminated by antioxidants in the body. However, upon injury, the balance between the ROS and the antioxidants present can be disturbed (Dwivedi et al., 2017; Min et al., 2018), leading to inflammation and prolonged wound healing. As such, an effective method for wound healing requires not only inhibiting the growth of bacteria, but also regulating abnormal inflammation (Liu et al., 2021).

Here, we designed cationic, multifunctional CDs, referred to as PAPBCDs, possessing both antibacterial and antioxidant properties, and investigated their ability to modulate inflammation and facilitate wound healing in vivo. The PAPBCDs were compounded of a simple one-step hydrothermal method from Sargassum australis polysaccharide (PAP) biomass and betaine (Scheme 1 ). As a naturally-occurring quaternary ammonium compound, betaine (N,N,N-trimethylglycine) is an N-methylated amino acid that can be obtained from dietary intake or can be biosynthesized from its metabolic precursor choline (Iravani and Varma, 2020), and has been shown that this method was beneficial to suppressing the development of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria when coated on CDs (Yang et al., 2016), whereas PAP has been shown to possess antioxidant activity. The resultant PAPBCDs were characterized by a combination of physical and spectroscopic methods, biochemical assays/free-radical scavenging studies were employed to confirm the antioxidant activity of these PAP-derived CDs, and their antibacterial performance was assessed by S. aureus and E. coli. Given the multifunctional properties imparted by the PAP (antioxidant) and betaine (antibacterial) precursors, in vivo tests were performed on a mouse trauma model infected with S. aureus to examine the multifunctional nanocomposite PAPBCDs' capacity to repair wounds. As will be demonstrated, the results demonstrate that the PAPBCDs not only retain the original antioxidant and antibacterial properties of their precursor starting materials, but are also able to reduce inflammation and wound healing outcomes in the infected in vivo wound model, suggesting that multifunctional PAPBCDs are promising nanomaterials for the treatment of bacterial wound infections.

Betaine hydrochloride (Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD, Shanghai > 99 %), NaCl (AR, Guangzhou, China), NaOH (AR, Xilong Scientific Co., LTD),2,2-azo-di-(3-ethylbenzodihydrothiazolin-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) (Aladdin, Shanghai), >99 %), 5,5-dimethyl-pyrroline n-oxide (DMPO) (analytical purity, Maclin, Shanghai), Fe2SO4 (AR, Guangzhou, China), and H2O2 (30 %, Guangzhou, China) were purchased and used without further purification. MRS broth, beef extract andbacteriology peptone were purchased from Guangdong Huankai Microbial Technology Co., LTD. Haikou People's Hospital (Hainan, China) provided the E. coli (ATCC 25922) and S. aureus (ATCC 25923). The Chinese Academy of Sciences' Cell Bank provided the RAW 264.7 cells. Thermo Fisher Technology (China) Co. LTD. was used to purchase DMEM medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), and penicillin–streptomycin (1 %) for the experiment. The Chinese company Tongren Chemical Technology Co., LTD. sold the Cell Count Kit-8 (CCK-8) to us.

The Padina australis raw material was gathered in Wanning City, Hainan Province, China's Nanyan Bay Beach. The PAP that was used in this study was created using our previously published methodology (Kholiya et al., 2021). Through ultrasonic-assisted extraction in diluted 1.0 M NaOH solution, PAP was successfully extracted. In a nutshell, 30 g of dry Padina australis were extracted in 0.04 M NaOH solution for 1 h in a water bath using ultrasonication, then boiled for 5 h in the same water bath. The supernatant was filtered, concentrated to 100 mL using a 3500 Da cutoff membrane, and then deproteinated using the Sevage method (Xiong et al., 2017) for 3 days using deionized water. The target PAP was made available by the dialyzed solution's lyophilization.

The PAPBCDs were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Briefly, 50 mg PAP and 100 mg betaine were dissolved into 20 mL deionized water to obtain the carbon dots precursor solution. The resulting mixture was put into an oven and cooked there for four hours at 200 °C. The resulting PAPBCDs were centrifuged to remove bigger precipitates after cooling to ambient temperature, and they were then dialyzed for 12 h against pure water using a dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da).

Using a JEM-2100 analytical electron microscope (JOEL, Ltd.), pictures from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were obtained. Infrared spectra were collected on a prepared KBr pellet containing the sample using a Smart Lab FTIR spectrometer (Nishiko Corporation, Japan), and XPS were collected on an Axis Supra X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Using an F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin Port East Science and Technology), photo-luminescence (PL) spectra were captured. A Beijing Puyang General Instrument TU-1901 spectrophotometer was used to record UV–vis absorption spectra. The Zetasizer Nano S90 (Malvern) was used to record assessments of the zeta potential and particle size.

The reaction of PAPBCDs with •OH was monitored by UV–visible spectroscopy. By subjecting an aqueous H2O2 solution to UV light, the following hydroxyl radicals were produced: 200 μL PAPBCDs were mixed with 1.8 mL H2O2 at different concentrations (0, 10, 50, 100, 250, and 500 mM), and the electronic absorption spectra were collected after incubation at room temperature for 0, 10, and 30 min.

A previously described technique was used to assess the radical scavenging capacity of ABTS (Kholiya et al., 2021). A 50 mL solution of 7 mM ABTS was combined with 0.89 mL of 140 mM potassium persulfate, and the mixture was then maintained at 4 °C for 12 h. This liquid was diluted to have an absorbance of 0.70 ± 0.02 at 734 nm before to the study's start. Then, 1.9 mL of the aforementioned ABTS solution was combined with 0.1 mL of the PAPBCDs at various concentrations (0, 20, 50, 100, 200 μg·mL−1) and left to react for 10 min in the dark. At 734 nm, absorbance spectra were taken.
Scavengingrate % =
1 -
A X
/
A b
× 100 %
where Ab is the absorbance of the control group (deionized water instead of the sample), and AX is the absorbance of the sample group.

Hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging activity was assayed by the Fenton reaction: a mixture of 50 μL, 0.5 mg·mL−1 PAPBCDs, DMPO (0.3 M, 50 μL), FeSO4 (10 mM, 50 μL), and 50 μL phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer (pH 7.4, 0.05 M) was prepared, reacted with H2O2 (5 mM) for 150 s, and the •OH free radical-scavenging activity was measured using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. The following ESR measurement conditions were used: 3512.3G is the central magnetic field (CF), 200G is the scanning width (SW), 15.36 s is the sweep time (ST), 3.0G is the modulation amplitude (MA), 9.863 GHz is the microwave frequency (MF), 20.0 mW is the microwave power (MP), and room temperature (T) is the test temperature (T).

The intracellular ROS scavenging activity of PAPBCDs was examined by co-culturing macrophages (RAW 264.7 cells) with various doses of PAPBCDs (0.2, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL) for 12 h, followed by an incubation with 100 M H2O2 for 2 h. A DCFH-DA test (Beyotime, China) was used to assess the level of intracellular ROS. The control group of cells were those that received no treatment.

The biocompatibility of the PAPBCDs was evaluated utilizing the CCK-8 assay. 96-well plates with different concentrations of PAPBCDs (0, 20, 50, 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 μg·mL−1) were seeded with 1 × 105 macrophages (RAW 264.7 cells). A 10 µL CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for an extra two hours after the initial 24 h. At 450 nm, an optical density (OD) reader (Biotek Synergy LX) was used to record the optical density (OD) results.

Gram-positive and Gram-negative model bacteria E. coli and S. aureus were used to examine the photothermal antibacterial efficiency of the PAPBCDs. The bottom of 96-well plates were filled with different concentrations of PAPBCDs (0, 20, 50, 100, 500, 1000, or 2000 μg·mL−1), and each plate received 80 μL of bacterial solution (1 × 107 CFU·mL−1). By measuring the number of colonies on agar plates, the PAPBCDs' antibacterial property was investigated. Each group's bacterial sample was 100-fold diluted before being spread out in 50 μL onto LB agar plates to grow for 24 h at 37 °C. Each experiment was performed in triplicate, and the colonies were counted after 24 h. The following formula was used to count the CFUs and determine the bacterial survival ratio: A = C / B × 100 %, where C represents the average CFUs of the experimental groups, B represents the average CFUs of the control samples (without PAPBCD treatment), and A represents the bacterium survival ratio.

The inactivated cells were seen by using propidium iodide to stain the bacteria. Propidium iodide (PI), 100 μg·mL−1, was used to stain bacterial cultures that contained or lacked PAPBCDs for 20 min while they were in the dark. The bacteria were examined using a LEICA SP8 laser scanning confocal microscope after being washed with PBS three times. Additionally, the wavelengths for excitation and emission were 535 nm and 615 nm, respectively.

The bacteria were collected after being treated with or without PAPBCDs, fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde, and then gradually dehydrated using a succession of ethanol solutions. After platinum sputter-coating, the samples were dried and analyzed by SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of wound healing in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The Animal Welfare and Ethical Review of Hainan University (Hainan, China) gave its clearance to all animal experiments with the approval code HNUAUCC-2021–00081. By making an 8 mm-diameter hole in the rear skin of Kunming male mice, the wound model was created. The PAPBCDs were subcutaneously injected into the infected areas after the wound was inoculated with 100 μL of S. aureus solution (108 CFU mL−1) for 24 h. The mice were subsequently split into two groups: the PAPBCDs-treated group and the PBS buffer-treated group (control). On the first, third, and tenth days following treatment, pictures of the affected areas were obtained. Last but not least, all mice were slaughtered for Masson and H&E stains.

Numerous physical and spectroscopic techniques were used to describe the synthesized PAPBCDs. The PAPBCDs were uniformly distributed, spherical in shape, and had an average particle size of about 9 nm, according to TEM images ( Fig. 1
a). A well-resolved lattice fringe with an intercrystalline spacing of 0.21 nm was visible using high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and corresponded to the graphite crystallographic plane (100). ( Fig. 1 b) (Lee et al., 2008). These results confirmed that carbon dots synthesized by this approach exhibited a graphitic structure.

The functional groups included in the PAPBCDs were examined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As seen in Fig. 1 c, the O-H stretching vibration and the asymmetric and symmetric C-H stretching vibrations, respectively, were responsible for the set of three FTIR absorption peaks for Padina australis, PAP, and betaine at 3450 cm−1, 3012 cm−1, and 2929 cm−1 (Geng et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2019). Additionally, the band at 1745 cm−1 was attributed to C = O vibrations, and the bands at 1039, 1406, and 1623 cm−1 were attributed to C-OH, C-NH, and N-H bonds, respectively, suggesting the existence of many hydrophilic groups on the surface of the PAPBCDs. Additionally, the absorption peak of −N+(CH3)3 in betaine was compatible with the band at 1479 cm−1 in the PAPBCDs, indicating that the compound was functionalized with a quaternary ammonium group. This band was attributed to the shear plane bending vibration of vibration of C-N in −N+(CH3)3. The fact that the PAPBCDs had a zeta potential of 5.43 ± 0.71 mV further demonstrated that they had a general positive charge compatible with the presence of quaternary nitrogen.

High resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) further confirmed the chemical bonds and functional groups of the PAPBCDs. The associated position (binding energy, eV) and intensity (normalized area) of the elements corresponding to the chemical formula of PAPBCDs are provided by the C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks (Fig. 1 d–f) and supporting curve-fitting analysis. (Contursi et al., 2020; Taner-Camcı and Suzer, 2014) Fig. 1 d shows the high resolution C1s peaks at 284.7 eV, which correspond to the graphene carbon's C − C/C = C bonds, together with peaks at 285.7, 286.9, and 288.6 eV, which correspond to the C − O/ C − OH (sp3 hybrid carbon), C − N (sp3 hybrid carbon), and C = O links, respectively. (Giesbers et al., 2013). In the case of N1s (Fig. 1 e), the peak at 394.7 eV was attributed to a C − N − C structure, while those at 401.3 eV and 407.7 eV were attributed to positively charged quaternary ammonium groups −N+(CH3)3 derived from the betaine starting material used in the synthesis of the PAPBCDs. The high-resolution O1s findings (Fig. 1 f) revealed maxima for C = O (carbonyl oxygen) bonds at 531.8 eV, C − O (sp2 hybrid oxygen) bonds at 533.1 eV, and O-H bonds at 534.2 eV (Datta et al., 2016). Taken together, quaternized PAPBCDs were successfully synthesized using a straightforward and effective procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of PAPBCDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
To ascertain the PAPBCDs' antioxidant capacities, their capacity to scavenge ABTS•+ and hydroxyl radicals was tested. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the ABTS•+ scavenging ratio rose from 0 to 72.9 % when the concentration of PAPBCDs was changed from 0 to 200 μg·mL−1, indicating a dose-dependent antioxidant activity. As shown in Fig. 2 b, when the Fe(II)-catalyzed Fenton reaction was used to generate •OH, (Li et al., 2020) a similar concentration-dependent antioxidant activity was also observed. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy is a direct and reliable method to identify and quantify free radicals in both chemical and biological environments. The increased ESR signal indicates a higher ROS generation rate (He et al., 2014; Lu et al., 2020). Therefore, as shown in Fig. 2, ESR signals were strongest when the PAPBCDs were absent. On the other hand, in the presence of PAPBCDs (0.2, 1, 2, 3, and 4 mg·mL−1), the ESR signal intensities were decreased in a dose-dependent manner by 39.2 %, 43.5 %, 53.1 %, 64.5 %, and 73.9 %, respectively. The relevant information has been updated accordingly. Taken together, the results shown in Fig. 2 indicated that PAPBCDs can effectively scavenge both ABTS•+ and •OH.

Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) exhibit strong oxidative activity, and can easily disrupt cell membranes and enter the interior where they interact with biomolecules and cause tissue damage (Ghaskadbi*, 2013). Thus, it is significant for protecting living systems from oxidative stress to remove •OH·H2O2 will produce •OH under ultraviolet irradiation, and the higher the hydrogen peroxide concentration, the more •OH will be produced (Ren et al., 2017). Here, the •OH scavenging activities of the PAPBCDs were further investigated. As show in Fig. 3 a, as the H2O2 concentration was increased, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, and the fluorescence showed a linear correlation with the increase concentrations of H2O2 (Fig. 3b) This could be explained by the fact that ·OH will react with the reductive groups (hydroxyl, aldehyde, etc.) on the surface of PAPBCDs, resulting changed the surface defects of CDs and then decline the fluorescence intensity. Therefore, with the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity decreased (Enguo Ju, 2014; Shao et al., 2022). Fig. 3c-e show the representative fluorescence spectral changes of PAPBCDs after UV light irradiation for 0, 10, 30 min in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2. The findings further confirmed that as illumination time was increased, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased.

During wound healing, excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals, and singlet oxygen, foster an inflammatory environment around the wound bed, luring additional inflammatory cells to the area, which prolongs wound healing (Fitzmaurice et al., 2011). Moreover, as strong oxidants, ROS can oxidize cellular components leading to cellular damage (Comino-Sanz et al., 2020). Therefore, mitigation of ROS is advantageous for wound healing, and the studies above demonstrate that PAPBCDs possess excellent antioxidant properties against ABTS•+ and hydroxyl radicals, and can potentially reduce ROS in vivo (Zuo et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assays and intracellular ROS scavenging
ce:section.ce:para:
For potential applications in the clinic, antimicrobial agents must be biocompatible with little side toxicity. Here, we used CCK-8 tests to gauge the PAPBCDs' cytotoxicity toward RAW 264.7 cells. As shown in Fig. 4
a, even at a high concentration (2000 μg·mL−1) of PAPBCDs, no statistically significant cell toxicity was observed (cell viability＞98 %).

Next, we investigated the effectiveness of the PAPBCDs in reducing intracellular ROS levels in H2O2-treated Raw 264.7 macrophages. As depicted in Fig. 4 b, using dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as the ROS-specific probe, confocal microscopy pictures were taken. (Lu Han1# and Tailin Guo1, 2019) showed that the fluorescence intensity of cells in the PAPBCDs-treated groups significantly reduced concentration-dependently when compared to those of the control. These results indicate that the PAPBCDs can serve an antioxidant role in vivo, effectively scavenging ROS generated from H2O2-stressed macrophages. We attribute the antioxidant activity of the PAPBCDs to two possibilities: i) PAP includes sulfuric and uronic acid groups, which are thought to be the main elements responsible for a polysaccharide's antioxidant activity. (Zhao et al., 2014); and ii), the abundance of surface-residing reducing groups (e.g., hydroxyl and aldehyde groups) on the PAPBCDs, which leads to their reaction with the ROS (He et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2014). Together, the findings imply that PAPBCDs might be used as a possible antioxidant agent for in vivo immunomodulation. (Liu et al., 2018; Shahid et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of PAPBCDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli were used to measure the PAPBCDs' antibacterial activity. (Fig. 5 ). As can be seen from the agar plate growth studies (Fig. 5 a) and their corresponding survival ability was calculated through counting of bacteria colony (Fig. 5 b), the PAPBCDs could efficiently decrease bacterial survival and inhibition rate of both E. coli and S. aureus by 99.38 % and 90.89 %, respectively. We surmise that the antibacterial activity of the PAPBCDs can be attributed to the cationic charges inherent to its quaternary ammonium surface groups, which causes damage to the negatively-charged bacterial cell membrane via electrostatic disruption, resulting in cell death (Zeng et al., 2020).

Propidium iodide (PI) fluorescent staining was used to further assess the PAPBCDs' antibacterial efficacy. PI is an impermeable membrane dye that fluoresces once bacterial cell membranes are damaged. Bacteria that exhibit a red fluorescence are regarded to be destroyed; hence, PI staining can clearly illustrate the antibacterial property of PAPBCDs, as opposed to a bacteriostatic mode of action. The confocal fluorescence microscopy images (Fig. 5 c) clearly depict that both pathogens significantly increased the amount of dead bacteria in the PAPBCDs-treated groups, consistent with the antibacterial activity observed in Fig. 5 a.

To further discussing mechanism of pathogen inactivation, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to investigate the morphological changes in the bacteria before upon PAPBCDs treatment (Fig. 5 d). Both pristine E. coli and S. aureus showed complete and smooth cell morphologies with continuous, undamaged cytoderm. However, upon exposure to the PAPBCDs, wrinkled or fragmented bacterial membranes were observed and the coarse surface of cytomembrane also showed some concave holes, indicating that the structure of the bacterial membrane had been severely damaged.

Altogether, these results provide unequivocal evidence confirming that the PAPBCDs can interact with the bacteria in a manner that leads to the rupture of their cell walls (Hsiao et al., 2015), and suggest that PAPBCDs with positively charged quaternary ammonium cations make potential antibacterial therapy candidates mainly because they are effective in vitro in killing both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of wound healing by PAPBCDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
The in vitro experiments demonstrated PAPBCDs has the antioxidant and antibacterial properties at the same time. In order to evaluate the therapeutic effect of the PAPBCDs on infected wound healing and skin regeneration in vivo, we established a mouse infection model with S. aureus. The infected mice were separated from two groups, namely, PBS buffer-treated (control) and PAPBCDs-treated groups. As shown in Fig. 6
a, on Days one, three and ten, the wound area images were taken to evaluate the healing process in relation to PAPBCDs treatment. While both groups showed wound healing after ten days, the PAPBCDs-treated group illustrated a statistical significace in powerful healing (Fig. 6 b).

Subsequently, as shown in Fig. 6 c and d, to determine whether PAPBCDs treatment improved the quality of the regenerated skin tissues, Masson's trichrome staining and H&E were both used (Hsiao et al., 2015). After 10 days, the control group had more inflammatory cells than the PAPBCDs-treated group (Fig. 6 c), demonstrating that PAPBCDs can effectively alleviate a bacteria-induced immune response during the inflammatory phase. Collagen deposition during wound healing signifies the regrowth of fresh skin tissues. Here, Masson’s staining revealed that collagen fibers in the PAPBCDs-treated group were even more dense, suggesting that PAPBCDs had a beneficial effect on the wound healing process. These results together suggest a potentially synergistic effect of antioxidant and antibacterial properties, as a result of the favorable effects of PAPBCDs therapy on collagen formation and wound microenvironment inflammation, skin tissue regeneration and wound healing are facilitated.

Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria possess a plethora of structural or secreted components that may stimulate host cells to activate a wide range of inflammatory responses. PAPBCDs with quaternary ammonium salt cation possess antibacterial activities. As a result, abnormal inflammatory responses can be managed by effectively eliminating bacteria. Additionally, reactive oxygen species (ROS) are involved in the initiation, progression, and resolution of the inflammatory response. PAPBCDs with a large number of reducing functional groups could effectively scavenge ROS. Therefore, mitigation of ROS is advantageous for inflammatory regulation and protect wound tissue from oxidative stress. Taken together, PAPBCDs with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties can modulate inflammation and promote tissue regeneration.

In summary, based on the concept of cooperative and synergistic effects, the multifunctional PAPBCDs were fabricated from antioxidant green biomass polysaccharides and antibacterial quaternary ammonium-containing betaine hydrochloride via a facile, one-step hydrothermal preparation method. The PAPBCDs retained the biological activity inherent in the raw materials, and exhibited excellent antioxidant and antibacterial activities. Specifically, the PAPBCDs retained polysaccharide antioxidant properties enabling the dynamic regulation of ROS. Furthermore, in vitro results demonstrated that the PAPBCDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility, but also outstanding anti-microbial activity of E. coli and S. aureus. Importantly, mouse infected wound models treated with PAPBCDs showed effective treatment of S. aureus infections in vivo with no concomitant tissue damage after prolonged tracking (10 days), thus verifying that these multifunctional CDs possessed combined effects of antioxidant and antibacterial activities supportive of tissue regeneration. This work not only provides a new strategy for preparing an unprecedented type of carbon dots, but it also further demonstrates that CDs have a potential for practical applications in wound healing.

Ju Lv: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hua Tian: Validation, Visualization. Lang Pan: Formal analysis, Methodology. Zihan Chen: Methodology. Mengting Li: Writing – review & editing. Reza A. Ghiladi: Writing – review & editing. Ziyu Qin: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xueqiong Yin: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Science and Technology Program of Haikou City (2021-024), Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (520QN224), College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project (S202210589012), and Key Research and Development Program of Hainan Province (GHYF2022001).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The antityrosinase and antioxidant activities of flavonoids dominated by the number and location of phenolic hydroxyl groups

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zuo, A.R., Dong, H.H., Yu, Y.Y., Shu, Q.L., Zheng, L.X., Yu, X.Y., Cao, S.W., 2018. The antityrosinase and antioxidant activities of flavonoids dominated by the number and location of phenolic hydroxyl groups. Chin Med 13, 51.
